name: Iroquois Confederacy continuity: Haudenosaunee
description: The Haudenosaunee (Iroquois Confederacy), founded c. 1142 by the Peacemaker (Deganawidah) and Hiawatha, initially uniting Five Nations (Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca). Joined by the Tuscarora (1722) as the Six Nations. The Great Law of Peace (Gayanashagowa) influenced American constitutional thought (Benjamin Franklin praised it explicitly). The confederacy persists today as legally recognized First Nations in Canada and tribal nations in the US.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Haudenosaunee's influence on the US Constitution is often understated — Franklin's 1751 letter and the 1744 Albany Congress explicitly drew from Iroquois federalism. The American Revolution (1775-1783) split the confederacy: Mohawks, Cayugas, Senecas, and Onondagas sided with Britain; Oneidas and Tuscaroras with the US — this internal breach was devastating. Post-1783, Haudenosaunee lands were massively reduced by US treaty violations (1784 Fort Stanwix, 1788 Fort Harmar, ongoing). The confederacy remains a sovereign nation with Haudenosaunee passports, though this sovereignty is contested by US and Canadian governments.

sources.citation: Richter, D. K. 'The Ordeal of the Longhouse' (1992)

links.description: Haudenosaunee ('People of the Longhouse'), the Iroquois Confederacy founded c. 1142 by the Peacemaker and Hiawatha. United Five Nations initially (Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca), joined by Tuscarora 1722. The Gayanashagowa (Great Law of Peace) is one of the earliest known codified constitutions. Continues today as sovereign nations in Canada (Six Nations of the Grand River) and the US.

name: Cherokee continuity: Tsalagi → Indian Territory diaspora
description: The Tsalagi (Cherokee) people, confederated from c. 1000 CE, developed a syllabary (Sequoyah, 1821), adopted a written constitution (1827), and established the Cherokee Nation under Principal Chief John Ross. Survived the 1838-1839 Trail of Tears forced removal (~4,000 deaths) to Indian Territory (Oklahoma). Today the Cherokee Nation (~400,000 enrolled members) is the largest federally recognized tribe in the US.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Cherokee removal is one of the best-documented cases of ethnic cleansing in US history — John Marshall's Supreme Court rulings (Worcester v. Georgia, 1832) found for the Cherokee, but Andrew Jackson's administration defied the Court. An estimated 4,000 of 16,000 Cherokee died on the Trail of Tears (1838-39). The Eastern Band of Cherokee (North Carolina) descends from those who evaded removal. Today's Cherokee Nation has its own courts, constitution, and citizenship — a continuing struggle for sovereignty.

sources.citation: McLoughlin, W. G. 'Cherokee Renascence in the New Republic' (1986)
Perdue, T. & Green, M. 'The Cherokee Nation and the Trail of Tears' (2007)

links.description: Tsalagi (Cherokee) confederation, formed c. 1000 CE in the southern Appalachian Mountains (modern Tennessee, North Carolina, Georgia). Adopted European governance forms in the early 19th century (written constitution 1827), only to be forcibly removed on the Trail of Tears (1838-39).

name: Muscogee / Creek Confederacy continuity
description: The Muscogee (Creek) Confederacy consolidated around 1540 CE as a response to Spanish incursions and epidemics. Comprised of towns (talwas) speaking Muskogean languages — Upper Creek (Alabama-Coosa rivers), Lower Creek (Ocmulgee-Flint). Sided with Britain in the American Revolution; internal split led to the Creek War (1813-14), then forced removal to Indian Territory 1832-1837. Today the Muscogee (Creek) Nation is one of the Five Tribes in Oklahoma.
ethical_notes: The 2020 Supreme Court ruling McGirt v. Oklahoma affirmed that much of eastern Oklahoma remains Muscogee reservation under federal law — a landmark for tribal sovereignty. The Creek War (1813-14), sometimes called the Red Stick War, was an internal civil war between traditionalists (Red Sticks) and accommodationists, used by Andrew Jackson to justify mass land cessions.

sources.citation: Saunt, C. 'A New Order of Things: Property, Power, and the Transformation of the Creek Indians, 1733-1816' (1999)

links.description: Muscogee (Creek) Confederacy, c. 1540-1832 in the Southeast US. Complex confederated structure of autonomous towns. Removed to Oklahoma 1832-37; sovereignty reaffirmed by McGirt v. Oklahoma (2020).

name: Lenape continuity: Lenapehoking
description: The Lenape (Delaware) people, homeland Lenapehoking covered modern New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Delaware. The 1682 Treaty with William Penn (the 'Walking Purchase' fraud of 1737) and subsequent dispossession drove most Lenape west. Today the Lenape diaspora spans Oklahoma (Delaware Nation, Delaware Tribe of Indians), Wisconsin (Stockbridge-Munsee), Ontario (Munsee-Delaware Nation, Moravian of the Thames, Delaware Nation at Moraviantown), with recent recognition movements in the ancestral homeland.
ethical_notes: The Walking Purchase (1737) is one of the most egregious colonial frauds — the Penn proprietors had long-distance runners cover 65 miles in 1.5 days to claim Lenape lands, invalidating oral agreements. The Manhattan 'sale' (1626) for 60 guilders is a famous oversimplification — the Lenape concept of land transfer differed fundamentally from European property transfer.

sources.citation: Kraft, H. C. 'The Lenape-Delaware Indian Heritage' (2001)

links.description: Lenapehoking, the Lenape homeland in the Delaware-Hudson-Raritan watersheds. Confederated politics among Unami, Unalachtigo, and Munsee language groups. Displacement began 1680s and culminated in forced removals 1830s-50s.

name: Diné (Navajo) continuity: Dine Bikeyah
description: The Diné (Navajo) people, arrived in the Southwest from the subarctic between 1100-1500. The Long Walk (1863-68) — the forced relocation of 8,500 Diné to Bosque Redondo (Fort Sumner), where 2,500 died — is the foundational trauma. The 1868 Treaty established the current reservation. Today the Navajo Nation (~400,000 enrolled) is the largest reservation in the US by area (71,000 km²).
ethical_notes: The Long Walk (1863-68) was orchestrated by Kit Carson's scorched-earth campaign (1863-64). Of ~10,000 forcibly walked, ~2,500 died at Bosque Redondo before survivors were allowed to return (1868 Treaty). The Navajo language's use by WWII Code Talkers is well-known; less acknowledged is the uranium mining on Navajo land (1944-86) that caused widespread illness and environmental contamination.

sources.citation: Iverson, P. 'Diné: A History of the Navajos' (2002)

links.description: Diné Bikéyah (Navajoland), the Diné homeland in the Four Corners region (Arizona, New Mexico, Utah, Colorado). Diné matrilineal clan-based society. Survived the Long Walk (1863-68) and returned to traditional lands.
